questions.question_html: If l, m, n are the direction cosines of a line, then
questions.explanation: We know that direction cosine is coefficient of i, j, k of unit vector along that line,i.e those coefficient are l,m,n the length of the line r is such that {tex}\overrightarrow r = l\widehat i + m\widehat j + n\widehat k{/tex} and magnitude of unit vector r is 1 and. {tex}\sqrt {{l^2} + {m^2}+{n^2}} {/tex} = 1 on squaring both side we get {tex}{l^2} + {\text{ }}{m^2} + {\text{ }}{n^2} = {\text{ }}1.{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}{l_1},{\text{ }}{m_1},{\text{ }}{n_1}{/tex} and {tex}{l_2},{\text{ }}{m_2},{\text{ }}{n_2}{/tex} are the direction cosines of two lines; and {tex}\theta {/tex} is the acute angle between the two lines; then find the angle between the lines?
questions.question_html: If {tex}{l_1},{\text{ }}{m_1},{\text{ }}{n_1}{/tex} and {tex}{l_2},{\text{ }}{m_2},{\text{ }}{n_2}{/tex} are the direction cosines of two lines; and {tex}\theta {/tex} is the acute angle between the two lines; then find the angle between the lines?
questions.explanation: If {tex}{l_1},{\text{ }}{m_1},{\text{ }}{n_1}{/tex} and {tex}{l_2},{\text{ }}{m_2},{\text{ }}{n_2}{/tex} are the direction cosines of two lines; and {tex}\theta {/tex} is the acute angle between the two lines; then the cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by : {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\left| {{l_1}{l_2} + {m_1}{m_2} + {n_1}{n_2}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {(l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2)(l_2^2 + m_2^2 + n_2^2)} }}{/tex} But {tex}{l_1^2 + m_1^2 + n_1^2 = 1}{/tex} and {tex}{l_2^2 + m_2^2 + n_2^2 = 1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \cos \theta = \left| {{l_1}{l_2} + {m_1}{m_2} + {n_1}{n_2}} \right|{/tex}

questions.explanation: If a 1 ,b 1 ,c 1, and a 2 ,b 2 ,c 2 are the direction ratios of two lines and {tex}\theta {/tex} is the angle between the two lines; then, the cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by: {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{{a_1}{a_2} + {b_1}{b_2} + {c_1}{c_2}}}{{\sqrt {a_1^2 + b_1^2 + c_1^2} \sqrt {a_2^2 + b_2^2 + c_2^2} }}} \right|{/tex} .

questions.explanation: The line passes through the points A(3, -2, -5) and B(3, -2, 6). {tex}\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} where {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} are position vectors of A, B respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=11 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} be the position vector of any point on the given line. {tex}\therefore{/tex} the vector equation vector of line is {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda(\vec{b}-\vec{a}){/tex} or {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}+11 \lambda \hat{k}{/tex} Taking {tex}\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{\jmath}+z \hat{k}{/tex} , the equation of line is {tex}x \hat{i}+v \hat{j}+z \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{\jmath}+(-5+11 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}{/tex} , we get, x = 3, y = -2, z {tex}=-5+11 \lambda{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x-3}{0}=\frac{y+2}{0}=\frac{z+5}{11}(=\lambda){/tex} Which is cartesian equation of line.

questions.explanation: If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the acute angle between {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{1}}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{2}}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}~{/tex} , then cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by : {tex}\cos \theta =\left| \frac{\overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}}.\overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}}{\left| \overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}} \right|\left| \overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}} \right|} \right|{/tex} Here, {tex}\overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}}=3\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+6\hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}=\hat{i}+2\hat{j}+2\hat{k}{/tex} Then, {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{(3\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k).(\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k)}}{{\left| {(3\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k)} \right|\left| {(\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k)} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{19}}{{\sqrt {49} \sqrt 9 }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{19}}{{21}}} \right| \Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\left| {\frac{{19}}{{21}}} \right|} \right){/tex}

questions.explanation: Given lines are: {tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{2p/7}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 3p/7}} = \frac{{y - 5}}{1} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 5}}{/tex} The D.R's of the lines are -3, 2p/7, 2 and -3p/7, 1, -5. {tex}\therefore \cos {90^o} = \frac{{( - 3) \times \left( {\frac{{ - 3p}}{7}} \right) + \left( {\frac{{2p}}{7}} \right) \times 1 + 2 \times ( - 5)}}{{\sqrt {9 + \frac{{4{p^2}}}{{49}} + 4} \sqrt {\frac{{9{p^2}}}{{49}} + 1 + 25} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{9p}}{7} + \frac{{2p}}{7} - 10 = 0 \Rightarrow p = \frac{{70}}{{11}}{/tex}

questions.explanation: On comparing the given equations with: {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_1}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_1}} ,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_2}} + \mu \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ,{/tex} we get: {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = \hat i - \hat j + \hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_2}} = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 2\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore S.D. = \left| {\frac{{(\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ).(\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} )}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{( - 3\hat i + 3\hat k).(\hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k)}}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{ - 3 + - 6}}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{ - 9}}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{3\sqrt 2 }}{2}{/tex}

questions.explanation: On comparing the given equations with: {tex}\overrightarrow{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{1}}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{{{b}_{1}}},{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{r}=\overrightarrow{{{a}_{2}}}+\mu \overrightarrow{{{b}_{2}}}{/tex} , we get: {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = \hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_2}} = 4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore S.D = \left| {\frac{{(\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ).(\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_2}} )}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{( - 9\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k).(3\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k)}}{{\sqrt {171} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{ - 27 + 9 + 27}}{{3\sqrt {19} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{9}{{3\sqrt {19} }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {19} }}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec r = \left( {1 - t} \right)\hat i + \left( {t - 2} \right)\hat j + (3 - 2t)\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\;\;\vec r = \left( {s + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {2s - 1} \right)\hat j - (2s + 1)\hat k{/tex}

questions.explanation: The given equations can be reduced as: {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+3\hat{k}+t\left( -\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2\hat{k}. \right){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}+s\left( \hat{i}+2\hat{j}-2\hat{k}. \right){/tex} On comparing them with: {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_1}} + t\overrightarrow {{b_1}} ,{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_2}} + t\overrightarrow {{b_2}} ,{/tex} We get: {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_2}} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = - \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore S.D = \left| {\frac{{(\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} ).(\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_2}} )}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{(2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k).(\hat j - 4\hat k)}}{{\sqrt {29} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left| {\frac{{ - 4 + 12}}{{\sqrt {29} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}} \right|{/tex}

questions.explanation: If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the acute angle between {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_1}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_1}} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{a_2}} + \lambda \overrightarrow {{b_2}}{/tex} then cosine of the angle between these two lines is given by: {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {{b_1}} .\overrightarrow {{b_2}} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} Here, {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = \hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k,\overrightarrow {{b_2}} = 3\hat i - 5\hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} Then, {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{(\hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k).(3\hat i - 5\hat j - 4\hat k)}}{{\left| {(\hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k)} \right|\left| {(3\hat i - 5\hat j - 4\hat k)} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{16}}{{\sqrt 6 \sqrt {50} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{16}}{{\sqrt {300} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\left| {\frac{{16}}{{10\sqrt 3 }}} \right|} \right){/tex} {tex}= {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\left| {\frac{8}{{5\sqrt 3 }}} \right|} \right){/tex}

questions.question_text: Determine whether the given planes 2x – y + 3z – 1 = 0 and 2x – y + 3z + 3 = 0 are parallel or perpendicular, and in case they are neither, find the angles between them.
questions.question_html: Determine whether the given planes 2x – y + 3z – 1 = 0 and 2x – y + 3z + 3 = 0 are parallel or perpendicular, and in case they are neither, find the angles between them.
questions.explanation: We have , 2x – y + 3z – 1 = 0 and 2x – y + 3z + 3 = 0. Here , {tex}\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \frac{{{b_1}}}{{{b_2}}} = \frac{{{c_1}}}{{{c_2}}} = 1{/tex} Therefore , the given planes are parallel.

questions.question_text: Determine whether the given planes 4x + 8y + z – 8 = 0 and y + z – 4 = 0 are parallel or perpendicular, and in case they are neither, find the angles between them.
questions.question_html: Determine whether the given planes 4x + 8y + z – 8 = 0 and y + z – 4 = 0 are parallel or perpendicular, and in case they are neither, find the angles between them.
questions.explanation: We have, 4x + 8y + z – 8 = 0 and y + z – 4 = 0. Let be the angle between the planes, then {tex}\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{{A_1}{A_2} + {B_1}{B_2} + {C_1}{C_2}}}{{\sqrt {A_1^2 + B_1^2 + C_1^2} \sqrt {A_2^2 + A_2^2 + A_2^2} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{4 \times 0 + 8 \times 1 + 1 \times 1}}{{\sqrt {16 + 64 + 1} \sqrt {0 + 1 + 1} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{9}{{\sqrt {18} \sqrt 2 }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right) \Rightarrow \theta = {45^o}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Find the distance of the point (0, 0, 0) from the plane 3x – 4y + 12 z = 3
questions.question_html: Find the distance of the point (0, 0, 0) from the plane 3x – 4y + 12 z = 3
questions.explanation: As we know that the length of the perpendicular from point {tex}P({{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}},{{z}_{1}}){/tex} from the plane {tex}{{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}z+{{d}_{1}}=0{/tex} is given by: {tex}\frac{\left| {{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}z+{{d}_{1}} \right|}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}_{1}+{{b}^{2}}_{1}+{{c}^{2}}_{1}}}{/tex} . Here, {tex}P(0,0,0){/tex} is the point and equation of plane is 3x – 4y + 12 z – 3. Therefore, the perpendicular distance is: {tex}\frac{\left| 0-0+0-3 \right|}{\sqrt{9+16+144}}=\frac{\left| -3 \right|}{\sqrt{169}}=\frac{3}{13}{/tex} units.

questions.question_text: Find the distance of the point (3, -2, 1) from the plane 2x - y + 2z + 3 = 0
questions.question_html: Find the distance of the point (3, -2, 1) from the plane 2x - y + 2z + 3 = 0
questions.explanation: As we know that the length of the perpendicular from point P(x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) from the plane a 1 x + b 1 y + c 1 y + d 1 = 0 is given by: {tex}\frac{{|{a_1}x + {b_1}y + {c_1}z + {d_1}|}}{{\sqrt {{a^2}_1 + {b^2}_1 + {c^2}_1} }}{/tex} Here, p(3, -2, 1) is the point and equation of plane is 2x - y + 2z + 3 = 0. Therefore, the perpendicular distance is: {tex}\frac{{|2(3) - ( - 2) + 2(1) + 3|}}{{\sqrt {4 + 1 + 4} }} = \frac{{|13|}}{{\sqrt 9 }} = \frac{{13}}{3}{/tex} Units

questions.question_text: Find the distance of the point (2, 3, -5) from the plane x + 2y - 2z = 9
questions.question_html: Find the distance of the point (2, 3, -5) from the plane x + 2y - 2z = 9
questions.explanation: As we know that the length of the perpendicular from point p(x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) from the plane a 1 x + b 1 y + c 1 z + d 1 = 0 is given by: {tex}\frac{{|{a_1}x + {b_1}y + {c_1}z + {d_1}|}}{{\sqrt {{a^2}_1 + {b^2}_1 + {c^2}_1} }}{/tex} Here, P(2, 3, -5) is the point and equation of plane is x + 2y - 2z = 9. Therefore, the perpendicular distance is: = {tex}\frac{{|2 + 2(3) - 2( - 5) - 9|}}{{\sqrt {1 + 4 + 4} }} = \frac{{|9|}}{{\sqrt 9 }} = \frac{9}{3}{/tex} = 3 units.

questions.question_text: Find the distance of the point (– 6, 0, 0) from the plane 2x – 3y + 6z – 2 = 0
questions.question_html: Find the distance of the point (– 6, 0, 0) from the plane 2x – 3y + 6z – 2 = 0
questions.explanation: As we know that the length of the perpendicular from point P(x 1 ,y 1 ,z 1 ) from the plane a1x +b1y +x1y + d1 = 0 is given by: {tex}\frac{{|{a_1}x + {b_1}y + {c_1}z + {d_1}|}}{{\sqrt {{a^2}_1 + {b^2}_1 + {c^2}_1} }}{/tex} Here, P(-6,0,0) is the point and equation of plane is 2x - 3y + 6y - 2 = 0. Therefore, the perpendicular distance is: {tex}\frac{{|2( - 6) + 0 + 0 - 2}}{{\sqrt {4 + 9 + 36} }} = \frac{{| - 14|}}{{\sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{14}}{7} = 2{/tex} units

questions.question_text: Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to 2x + 3y + 4z – 12 = 0
questions.question_html: Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to 2x + 3y + 4z – 12 = 0
questions.explanation: Direction ratio’s of the line are ( 2, 3, 4 ). Therefore, the equation of the line is: {tex}\frac{{x - 0}}{2} = \frac{{y - 0}}{3} = \frac{{z - 0}}{4} = \lambda {/tex} Thus, the coordinates of any point P on the above line are P ( 2 {tex}\lambda {/tex} , 3 {tex}\lambda {/tex} , 4 {tex}\lambda {/tex} ). But , this point P also lies on the given plane: 2(2 {tex}\lambda {/tex} ) + 3(3 {tex}\lambda {/tex} ) + 4(4 {tex}\lambda {/tex} ) – 12 = 0. {tex} \Rightarrow 29\lambda = 12 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{12}}{{29}}{/tex} Therefore, the coordinates of the foot of perpendicular are given by: {tex}\left( {2 \times \frac{{12}}{{29}},3 \times \frac{{12}}{{29}},4 \times \frac{{12}}{{29}}} \right){/tex}

questions.question_text: Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to 3y + 4z – 6 = 0
questions.question_html: Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the origin to 3y + 4z – 6 = 0
questions.explanation: Direction Ratio’s of the line are < 0 , 3 , 4 > . Therefore, equation of the line is : {tex}\frac{{x - 0}}{0} = \frac{{y - 0}}{3} = \frac{{z - 0}}{4} = \lambda {/tex} Thus, the coordinates of any point P on the above line are P ( 0, 3 {tex}\lambda {/tex} , 4 {tex}\lambda {/tex} ). But , this point P also lies on the given plane : 0 + 3(3 {tex}\lambda {/tex} ) +4(4 {tex}\lambda {/tex} ) – 6 = 0. {tex} \Rightarrow 25\lambda = 6 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{6}{{25}}{/tex} Therefore, the coordinates of the foot of perpendicular are given by : {tex}\left( {0,3 \times \frac{6}{{25}},4 \times \frac{6}{{25}}} \right)i.e.\left( {0,\frac{{18}}{{25}},\frac{{24}}{{25}}} \right){/tex}
